sentences.text: Yeah.
My name is Hanna Lifschutz.
L-I-F-S-C-H-U-T-Z.
Hanna, H-A-N-N-A.
Rubin, R-U-B-I-N.
No.
Yes, the nickname is called [?
Hanukkah ?].
It's a Polish abbreviation--
--For Hanna, yes.
Second, second, 1920.
Unfortunately, because the beginning of the month, and I'm 77 now.
Yeah.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
Well, we were a typical Jewish, middle-class family.
My father came from a rather religious tradition, a religious family.
And they were, they were, they were wealthy people, living-- they had, they had land.
And I was actually brought up there, till the age of six.
My mother died when I was one year old, and my father was devastated.
And my sister, who is eight years older than I am, went to live with my grandparents-- my mother's parents.
And I was with a, with a Polish girl, not an English nanny, but a Polish, nice, Polish, English peasant.
I was taken to my father's grandparents, who lived Makow, Makow.
In Poland, in [NON-ENGLISH].
And she was actually with me till I, till I, till I was about six or seven years old, and then she used to come.
And I grew up with my other grandparents-- very, very free in the country, with the good food and good care, till I was six.
And then my father married again, a widow.
She was a young woman, about 12 years younger than my father.
She came from [NON-ENGLISH].
Her name was actually [?
Rouge ?], but we called her Rosa, because simplified.
And she was, study-- she was with her first husband in Germany, in Breslau and Koeningsberg, where he studied medicine.
And there where her little boy, Rudolf, was born.
When I came to live with my father again and my stepmother, the little boy, Rudolf, was already there, and I simply adored him.
He was two years older than I.
And my father-- after a few years, my half-brother was born who was called Michael, and we called him [?
Mikash ?].
And we had, we had-- my sister was still with my grandparents.
But being older, she-- and she used to, she used to visit us regularly.
We had a little, a very nice country house, which is not a villa in the Tuscany.
[LAUGHS] But it was a very nice, wooden building and quite a lot of land with it.
And we spent the childhood going from Warsaw by electric, train 20 minutes away from-- and we had a very, very lovely time over there.
And I went with my daughter and my son-in-law, Peter Lawrence.
I went to Warsaw with them in 1995.
And I, and I found this villa, this only building.
The land was went into parcellation and the building-- and I have it in a photograph, too.
It's amazing.
Well, that was that.
And then my father was-- owned a mill.
He-- because his parents and grandparents, they were actually mill owners and they were-- they had-- they were experts on forestry.
And so he, he had this.
And that's why we lived on this [NON-ENGLISH] street, because it was to train to Milanowek, where he had this--
In Warsaw-- outside Warsaw.
The mill was outside Warsaw.
Yeah, yeah.
We lived in Warsaw.
We lived in Warsaw till, actually, actually, till two years before the war.
Then we moved into [? father ?] state, which, by coincidence, became the small ghetto.
But in this respect, we were very lucky.
But that was that.
And that was-- I went to school, you know?
Went to, first, the school which all the Polish children went compulsory till seven.
And then I went to a Jewish gymnasium called [NON-ENGLISH].
And I loved the school.
I met nice friends, lovely friends.
And I enjoyed learning.
And that was-- I finished in '38.
I was yet not quite certain what I would do.
Probably would-- having the opportunity, maybe I would have studied, probably would, some kind of-- because I liked learning.
And there was the war.
Yes.

We were, we were, we were absolutely-- [? and ?] although, we lived for 16, 17 years in the Warsaw, in Warsaw in the part which was not predominately Jewish at all, we were absolutely acknowledged Jews.
And my father returned regularly to synagogue.
Of course, we went--
It was a local synagogue, near our place.
Occasionally, on very big occasions, we went to the [NON-ENGLISH] synagogue, which was one of the greatest-- really beautiful, which was unfortunately burned down.
In Warsaw.
And my two brothers, who went to gymnasium, and my other brother, actually, stepbrother, actually finished gymnasium very successfully and went to studies.
And the little one who was still small, we were regularly to the synagogue.
And we were also Zionist-orientated, though Palestine, at that time, was very remote, this place.
But we were remotely associated because of my father's uncle, was Nahum Sokolow.
Nahum Sokolow.
Nahum Sokolow.
And he-- it was not-- my grandmother and Nahum Sokolow's wife were sisters.
So it was not a very close association, but I didn't meet him ever.
My sister did, I think.
And my, my father, at a certain time of life-- must have been '33 or before, maybe before, when he occasionally came to Warsaw and asked him if we could emigrate to Palestine.
And he used, he used-- he said famously, [NON-ENGLISH].
Wherever you-- if you have a good way of living, stay, because life in Palestine is very hard.
And later on, I met this Sokolov family when I returned from India, and we became very great friends with the elderly, Selena and Florian.
Not Jewish, no.
No.
And the other one was Jewish.
Was Jewish.
The secondary was Jewish school, yes.
Yeah, yes.
Only for girls, only for girls.
Yes.
We had a lot of friends, and very lively life.
And we had like-- we were five close, very close friends, about six, seven boys-- some of them were already at the university.
One I met in the ghetto afterwards, was-- played a very predominant part in my life.
And we were-- I don't know.
We were, you know, we were not rich.
But we were well-off, and we were-- we were able to enjoy, let's say, going to concerts, for 50 [INAUDIBLE], right up to the-- nearly to the ceiling.
And going to the theater sometimes, and enjoying talking and discussing things.
And read books, and met people, and school meetings, and out-of-school meetings.
We had a very lively life, a very normal life for girls of, of my age.
Most of my friends were Jewish, but in the locality we lived, we had quite a few non-Jewish neighbors, and they were extremely charming and nice.
And personally, I myself, never encountered this antisemitism in those days.
This years of my school years.
Yes.

But unfortunately, my elder-- my stepbrother, who we adored and loved, went to university to study medicine because his father was a [? doctor, ?] otherwise wouldn't he be able to, to attend the faculty.
And they had-- the Jewish boys had to stand on the lectures.
They were not allowed to sit.
And there was a rampant antisemitism in Poland already.
Lectures, yeah.
They were not allowed to sit down because they were Jewish.
And then, of course, he didn't finish this because of the war.
And yes.
My sister-- when I mention my little brother, [?
Mikash ?], who also went to the same, it was called [NON-ENGLISH] Gymnasium.
It was excellent gymnasium.
And he was still a young boy.
And we enjoyed him at home, because lovely.
My sister was already-- she went to a very good school called [NON-ENGLISH], which was one of the prominent, very Jewish school.
And she lived with my grandparents.
And we saw her occasionally.
She used to come, and oh, we went to this little villa, you know?
And she visited us.
We had a very good relationship in that-- with my stepmother.
She was a very nice, very nice lady.
Unfortunately-- very nice.
Died so terribly, you know?
Probably, I don't know even how.
And she was, she was a very good woman.
And it was, it was a nice harmony at home.
Mhm.
It was a good Jewish home, a Jewish home.
Holidays--
Yes.
Every, every Jewish holiday, because my father was an orthodox.
But he was not a strictly ortho-- very ortho.
He was orthodox, but not extreme, what we call today.
Not at all.
He was quite tolerant with us children.
But holidays were beautifully at home.
They were-- you know, in Polish-- in Jewish custom, it was that-- to every marriage, you brought a beautiful set of silver candelabras.
So there was my mother's silver candelabras.
There was my stepmother's candelabras, and they bought together.
There was six on the table.
There was beautiful tablecloths and silver.
And that traditional Jewish way, my father leaning on the left to the on Pesach.
And everything had to be Kosher and done properly, another set of things, crockery, and cutlery and whatever.
And so it went every-- and we children used to have clothes, new clothes.
And we had to go to bed and then sleep, which we couldn't sleep, because too excited.
And then we put the new clothes and the lovely patent shoes.
And made-- each time we walked, made such special noise.
And we were delighted.
Rapped it with our hands not to make a spot on the shoe.
It was a terrific excitement, the holiday.
Especially the Pesach.
Pesach.
And The new year.
Yes, yes.
We-- my stepmother-- my sister came, or she was with my grandmother.
It was a vast family.
Many, many members of the-- my, my wonderful Auntie [?
Lutka ?], who we adored absolutely.
And she was with my grandmother and my grandfather.
My sister spent sometimes her holidays there.
My stepmother had a vast family who used to visit us regularly.
And we children sometimes resented it and played all kinds of tricks.
Because, you know, like, in the olden days, the, the, the relatives didn't go to any hotel.

They just, just embarked on your place, and-- but the feeling of security was tremendous, because we children never felt-- there was not this dreadful feeling of taking drugs, which is, to my mind, lack of security and break of the family.
And in Jewish families, it was a bastion of strength, the Jewish family.
We shared bar mitzvahs, and weddings and all kinds of-- simchat.
And we had, sometimes, unhappiness, or sometimes adventures, or was sometimes scandalous things.
But it was all dealt within the family unit.
And it was a blessing, because a child-- if mother and father was not at home, they went to the auntie, then went to the grandmother.
And a feeling of security was a very prominent aspect of, of, of our Jewish life.
Yes, yes.
Oh yes.
We had the, the, we had the melamed, called melamed--
--Who, my father-- it means a teacher who insisted-- he came over to teach us Hebrew.
And we were very naughty.
Sometimes we hid under the table and under the-- behind the cupboards, and we didn't want to.
We didn't want to because it was a different language.
And we children felt, we want-- we don't want-- but I did learn my alphabet then and there.
And have a vague way.
And, of course, I can, I can, I can read the prayers and, and, and, a bit of modern Hebrew.
I tried many times.
Mostly Jewish.
The Jewish people in Poland-- it was a special feature.
The Jewish people of Poland were-- you know, they-- many of them who lived in the predominantly Jewish precinct, they, they, even-- many of them didn't speak Polish.
They spoke only, only Yiddish.
We, at home, spoke, of course, Polish, because this was the languages my father also needed.
And my, my Yiddish was, of course, the Yiddish language, which I know very well today.
And then it was-- my stepmother spoke fluently German, because she spoke with her, with her-- which was in Germany.
And then my brother took German at his, for his matriculation.
And French at school.
But Russian, even.
My father came from old Russian school.
So all these languages were familiar.
Though not all of them were perfectly spoken, I should say.
But they were familiar.
But the Jewish people in Warsaw, because of the religion, they rather spoke Yiddish, and Hebrew was the language of the holy, holy, holy our holy books.
Oh, yes.
Due to my good school, this was, this was one of the elements of my luck.
And later on, because of my good Polish pronunciation.
Then war broke out.
War broke out--
We knew already.
Already there were signs.
And, of course, everybody knew [INAUDIBLE]
We knew Hitler was coming to the power--
--In Germany.
He won, and he would be the chancellor.
And we knew his We knew his, his-- we knew his plans, how to deal with, with, with, his own society and with the Jewish society living in Germany.
And were premonitions-- very, very, very, very bad premonitions of forthcoming, you know, uncertainty and so on.
But then the war broke out and the Germans, of course-- the first impact.
The first brutal, unbelievable, brutal, impact of German forces came to Poland.
And Poland, in their innocence and ignorance, believed that they can defend itself.
Of course, the war was finished in a few weeks.
In two, three weeks, the war was over.
But the bombardment of Warsaw was fierce.
And we were in the shadows.
We never knew who had survived, who has not survived, whether which house was hit.
Which-- that was very traumatic experience.
No, no.
We were, we were, we were in the shelters--
In the shelter of your house.
Of our own house, yes.
Afterwards, we were very lucky, because with minor damages, the flat was in order.
And we all were not wounded or anything like that.
It was-- there were very bad times, because, you know, the rubble on the street, and the smell of the burning houses, and wood, and whatever.
And then the Germans came, of course, and immediately started with the pronouncements, what has to be done, you know.

One of the first ones, the first order for the, for the, for the, for the public, and they formed straightaway some sort of, some sort of working forces-- not only Jewish, but everybody-- to, to, to clean the rubble and clean the, the, the streets.
And then there were pronouncements of-- there were, there were, very quite short time afterwards that the Jews have to be prepared themselves.
Actually, took now to 1940, till it was-- we still lived in our own flat and all that things.
But in 1940 sometime, there were already pronouncements that to the Jewish people, who in majority occupied the north of Warsaw, which was [NON-ENGLISH] and all sorts of things--
Which were completely-- almost completely occupied.
But not all.
There were quite a lot of Christian people who lived there, Polish people.
So that was organized in this way that the Polish people who lived there had to, had to abandon the flats or houses.
They were allowed to take all the [INAUDIBLE] with them, whatever they had.
Because they had-- the Polish people, the Christian people, had to move to the different part of Poland, because the, the north of Warsaw, the Germans decided to make this a ghetto.
A ghetto was to be formed in the north of Warsaw, where predominantly, Jewish people lived there.
And those Christians who was there, they have to move out into different part, which was not such a hardship, because they occupied the good Jewish houses, which were left with everything.
The Jews, to the contrary of the Christians, were allowed only to take a few bundles with them, only the personal clothing.
Nothing else.
Nothing else.
And that, that, that was they key.
And then you have seen on the streets, you know, this very heartbreaking situation.
Pictures of Jews pushing carts without horses, with all the chattels and the things falling down, and the little children weeping and crying, sometimes being lost.
This was quite a long period throughout 1940, till the resettlement took place.
So the Christians went to the proper part of Warsaw, and the Jewish people moved into northern parts.
But the, the Germans organized the ghettos in such a way.
They formed two ghettos.
It's not always well-known that there were two ghettos.
But there were two ghettos.
One was--
In Warsaw.
Which, we were in this respect, lucky because we moved from [NON-ENGLISH] street, where we lived for 16, 17 years, into [NON-ENGLISH] street.
Two years before the war.
I don't know why, I don't remember why.
We thought that we might change for a bigger flat, and something-- my family was larger, and so on.
And this small ghetto consisted of a few streets only.
As far as I remember, was [NON-ENGLISH], was [NON-ENGLISH], and was one, two other street.
And it was a very small ghetto.
Then was like a no man's land they created.
And then was this new ghetto, started from Leshno street.
From Leshno street up to [NON-ENGLISH], up to the absolute north of Warsaw, that was absolutely Jewish part, and that was the ghetto.
Now, what they announced is that there will be formed shops.
We didn't know at that time what were shops.
English was not often spoken in Poland.
Shops were--
[NON-ENGLISH] Shops.
[NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], and various other shops in those [INAUDIBLE].
The shops which were formed in the small ghetto, where I was, was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And that was mainly stitching uniforms for the Wehrmacht.

And the big ghetto, of course, since I was not living there, I wouldn't be able to give an exact account, but my little brother, I secured a place in one big electric, electric-- that was an establishment, a big firm, before the war.
And I managed to, to, to register him there.
And they had everything.
They had shoe making, and electricity.
And clothes, and brushes for cleaning horses, I believe.
And boots, hats.
And, of course, a big jewelry department, because they robbed Jews of all their--
--Whatever belongings they had.
And, of course, Jewish people in Warsaw left all the pictures, and all the beautiful furniture, and carpets, everything.
And even clothes.
And we were not allowed to take fur coats.
I don't know if it is ever known.
We were not allowed to take any fur coats or wear any piece of fur on our clothes.
All fur.
Any fur.
Nothing.
Bare.
That had to be left.
So they inherited the fur coats, and they inherited everything.
And that was the beginning.
I was-- no, no, no.
I was in the small ghetto.
My whole family-- let's say, like my grandmother, my beloved auntie, and uncles, and cousins, and whatever, were in the big ghetto.
And even small-- my youngest brother, I secured-- because I couldn't secure for him any place-- my-- in the big ghetto.
Now, I would like to add that my stepbrother was taken-- you know--
He developed tuberculosis.
He was with us, still in 1940, in our flat.
And he developed tuberculosis.

And with our very good old friend, a doctor, we managed-- particularly, because my parents wouldn't and be able to-- we managed to smuggle him out, outside the-- we were only in the ghetto, but it was not yet completely closed, you see?
So we smuggled him out to a place called Otvosk, which was on the same line as our little villa.
Our villa was in [NON-ENGLISH], where we had the villa, and that was Otvosk.
And that was known for-- there was no penicillin for curing tuberculosis, but it was-- pine tree were there.
And lot of pine.
The good air, and a sanatorium.
So we smuggled him there.
And the kindly doctors and nurses accepting him, knowing, even knowing, that he was a Jew.
OK, we're coming now to the end of the first tape.
Yeah.
